creator: cse.cem@gmail.com
text:
(In reply to Ed Maste from comment #8)
> I've tested Conrad's v2 patch and can confirm it works.

Good :).

> Conrad, to be clear, you mention that this was fixed in the GPLv3 version,
> but your implementation here is developed independently, correct?

Correct. The patch is (and v1, and the pasted fragment are) my own work, derived from grokking / modifying / testing the GPLv2 version of binutils in CURRENT.

(In reply to Ed Maste from comment #9)
> Also, are there any cases where the name won't match the lib<foo>.so<.bar>
> pattern, perhaps resulting in a confusing message?

It's possible — I think you can set the SONAME field to basically anything you want. E.g. here's the status quo of DT_SONAME in lld(1) (anything caller specifies ends up byte-for-byte in DT_SONAME field):

http://lists.cs.uiuc.edu/pipermail/llvm-commits/Week-of-Mon-20130909/188011.html

And I wouldn't be surprised if GNU binutils were similar.

I just spend about an hour trying to find something spelling out the lib<foo>.so<.bar> convention, and couldn't find anything saying so. It *appears* that DT_SONAME is simply a filename hint, copied into binaries' DT_NEEDED sections by the linker, for the dynamic loader. Obviously it is a very common convention, though. The convention is used in the System V ABI doc[0] as well as Linux shared-libraries docs[1].

I don't expect bizarre sonames are particularly common.

[0]: http://www.sco.com/developers/devspecs/gabi41.pdf 6-3 / page 104
[1]: http://tldp.org/HOWTO/Program-Library-HOWTO/shared-libraries.html
